---
title: "Compute"
---

A surprising amount of low-level computing is circular: ring buffers wrap, work queues round-robin, and array layouts tile by a fixed stride. This page shows how the S1 vocabulary makes those patterns *checkable* — you can prove the addressing is safe, even though you cannot prove it is fast.

<span class="showcase-backlink" data-showcase-ref="SHOW-011"></span>

::: {.lesson-goal}

## Lesson Goal

See four computing patterns — circular buffers, direction bins, stride layouts, and round-robin schedules — as finite circles, and understand exactly what "proved" buys you here (address safety and closure) versus what it does not (speed).

:::

## The Idea

Each pattern is a circle `C_n` with a stride:

- A **ring buffer** of size `n`: writing at position `i` lands at `i mod n`.
- **Direction bins**: a ray's index lands in one of `n` bins.
- A **stride layout**: element `k` lands at `(k · stride) mod n`.
- A **round-robin schedule**: task `t` goes to worker `t mod n`.

The proved seed says the address *wraps correctly and closes after a full pass*. That is real — off-by-one and overflow bugs are exactly what it rules out. It says nothing about cache locality or throughput; those are benchmark questions.

**Two honest tiers, as everywhere in the applications.** The `COMP*` theorems themselves
(an index stays in range, closes after a full pass, normalizes idempotently) are
**elementary modular-index bookkeeping** — the same finite-closure family as the cyclic-memory
facts in the AI lane. They are honest and useful, but not deep. The one genuinely
non-trivial structural fact here is *coverage*: a stride layout visits **every** cell iff
the stride is coprime to `n` — which is not a separate result but the very same proved
coverage rule as strided attention, `AIT-T0002`. That is where the orbit/gcd theory does
real work.

## Worked Example

A ring buffer of size `8`, writing a stream of indices:

```text
write 6  -> slot 6 mod 8 = 6
write 13 -> slot 13 mod 8 = 5
write 16 -> slot 16 mod 8 = 0   (back to the start: one full lap)
```

And a round-robin over `4` workers:

```text
task 6 -> worker 6 mod 4 = 2
task 7 -> worker 7 mod 4 = 3
task 8 -> worker 8 mod 4 = 0   (the schedule closes and repeats)
```

Both are just `C_n` rotation. The closure facts (`adding a full size changes nothing`) are what the cards below certify.

## Common Mistake

::: {.common-mistake}

Proving an address wraps correctly is not proving a kernel is fast. The CoilLayout/stencil fixtures (`COMPL-B0001`, `COMPL-B0002`) are executable references for expected outputs — they are not performance claims, and they do not prove cache locality, stencil correctness, or backend speed. See [What "Proved" Means Here](../../what_proved_means.qmd).

:::

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

In a ring buffer of size `8`, where does `write 20` land, and after how many more writes does the slot return to that position? (Answer: slot `4`; every `8` writes.)

:::

## Source Trail

<p data-showcase-ref="SHOW-011">Showcase evidence: <a href="../../showcase.qmd#SHOW-011">SHOW-011 circular compute layouts and schedules</a>.</p>

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0002"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPC-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPC-T0004"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPC-T0005"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPR-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPR-T0004"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPR-T0005"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPL-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPL-T0005"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPS-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="COMPS-T0004"></div>

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0040"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0045"></div>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_COMP_01_PROOF_CARRYING_CIRCULAR_COMPUTATION.md">Proof-carrying compute paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_COMP_01_PROOF_CARRYING_CIRCULAR_COMPUTATION.md">Circular Computation</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_COMP_03_COIL_LAYOUT_STENCIL_NTT.md">Layout paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_COMP_03_COIL_LAYOUT_STENCIL_NTT.md">Coil Layout, Stencil, And NTT</a></p>

<div class="book-navigation">
<a href="generative.qmd">Previous: Generative Structures</a>
<a href="rendering.qmd">Next: Rendering</a>
</div>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
